clean_story:
Thoughts as silent gestures. The brain’s hidden choreography.

The phrase thought_as_bodily_motion treats ideas not as abstract sparks but as physical movements—tiny, measurable actions inside the brain. Like a pianist’s fingers pressing keys without sound, neurons fire in patterns that are thinking.

Here’s how it works: When you imagine kicking a ball, your motor cortex lights up almost identically to when you actually kick it. fMRI scans show this "mental rehearsal" tricks the brain into practicing without movement. Athletes use it—studies prove visualizing free throws improves accuracy by 23% over no practice. Even phantom limbs, lost to amputation, can "move" when patients think hard enough; their brain still sends signals to muscles that aren’t there.

The body doesn’t just follow thoughts—it is them. A 2018 study found people solving math problems unconsciously tilted their heads left (abstract thinking) or right (concrete details). Your posture, eye darting, even breath rhythm leak cognition like ink in water.

Three related subjects are mirror neuron mysteries, embodied artificial intelligence, and how dance rewires the brain.
